ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai iced milk tea with jelly; 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03311737) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0EJW (tea-based beverage, sweetened, with milk) is the closest defensible code; no specific code exists for Thai iced milk tea with jelly inclusions.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Thai iced milk tea with jelly; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: Thai red tea (Ceylon or Thai blend)

serving.natural_language_portion: One regular cup (approximately 400 g) is the standard commercial serving size for this Thai iced milk tea with red tea jelly, as listed on the GrabFood platform.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — component estimation from brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917), sweetened condensed milk (FDC ID 172185), evaporated milk (FDC ID 172186), and granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655); cross-validated against Singapore HPB Foodo database for tea-based beverage category
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Thai iced milk tea with red tea jelly. Nutrient values are estimated from the input record (GrabFood-derived, confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) and cross-validated for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central component entries for brewed black tea (FDC ID 171917), sweetened condensed milk (FDC ID 172185), evaporated milk (FDC ID 172186), and granulated sugar (FDC ID 169655). The input values are internally consistent with a typical Thai iced milk tea formulation (approximately 40% brewed tea, 20% condensed milk, 20% evaporated milk, 5% sugar, 15% jelly/ice) and are retained as the best available estimate. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not contain a direct entry for this specific commercial product. USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved secondary global source per Project Veritas v2 source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database — sweetened condensed milk tea analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai iced milk tea with red tea jelly. GI estimated at 55 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened milk tea and condensed milk tea analogues, which report GI values in the range 50–60. The dominant glycemic driver is sucrose from condensed milk and added sugar (GI ~65) diluted by the liquid matrix and milk proteins, yielding a blended estimate of approximately 55. GI 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI Database classification (low: GI ≤ 55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 42.0) / 100 = 23.1. Carbohydrate per serving = 10.5 g/100g × 400 g = 42.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (9.5 g/100 g) in a sugar-sweetened beverage exceeds the 5 g/100 g beverage sugar gate; independent blood pressure risk from SSB consumption regardless of low sodium (30 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this beverage due to its high sugar content (9.5 g/100 g); if consumed, request an unsweetened version with condensed milk replaced by plain low-fat milk, and limit portion to no more than 150 ml.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage with 9.5 g sugar/100 g and negligible dietary fibre (0.13 g/100 g); condensed milk and added sugar cause rapid postprandial glucose excursion with a high glycemic load of 23.1 per 400 g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage as it is a sugar-sweetened drink with high sugar (9.5 g/100 g) and very low fibre (0.13 g/100 g), producing a high glycemic load of 23.1 per serving; if desired, request plain unsweetened black tea without condensed milk or jelly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat (1.75 g/100 g) from condensed milk and evaporated milk combined with high added sugar (9.5 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit or avoid this beverage due to its saturated fat content (1.75 g/100 g) and high added sugar (9.5 g/100 g) from condensed milk; substituting with plain unsweetened black tea eliminates both risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Condensed milk contributes saturated fat and refined sugar, both pro-inflammatory; added sugar further promotes inflammatory mediator production
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this beverage due to its pro-inflammatory profile from condensed milk (saturated fat and refined sugar) and added sugar; plain unsweetened black tea is preferred to access polyphenol benefits without the inflammatory burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: CT3 Cha Red Tea Jelly is a Thai-style iced milk tea beverage sold commercially in Singapore, featuring strongly brewed Thai red tea (typically a Ceylon or Thai-blend tea with added food colouring and spices) mixed with sweetened condensed milk, evaporated milk, and sugar, then chilled and served over ice with chewy red tea jelly pieces. The drink has a rich, creamy, and intensely sweet flavour profile with a characteristic orange-amber colour and a smooth, milky texture punctuated by the soft, bouncy texture of the red tea jelly. It is a popular Southeast Asian street-food-style beverage adapted for the Singapore commercial market. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 75 kcal, 1.13 g protein, 3.0 g fat (1.75 g saturated), 10.5 g carbohydrate, and 9.5 g sugar, with low dietary fibre (0.13 g) and moderate sodium (30 mg). The high sugar content and negligible fibre make this a high-glycemic-load beverage (GL 23.1 per 400 g serving, GI estimated 55). It is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its sugar-sweetened beverage classification, high added sugar from condensed milk, and saturated fat contribution. The beverage is halal, vegetarian, and gluten-free, but contains dairy allergens from condensed and evaporated milk.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are derived from the GrabFood-sourced input record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) and cross-validated for plausibility against USDA FoodData Central component entries for brewed black tea, sweetened condensed milk, and evaporated milk. No exact approved-source entry exists for this specific commercial product. Values are internally consistent with the described formulation but cannot be verified against a primary laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo direct entry. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific Thai iced milk tea with red tea jelly formulation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-17. 2. potassium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific commercial formulation are not available in approved sources as of 2026-04-17; USDA FoodData Central consulted and confirmed unavailability for this composite product. 3. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database sweetened milk tea entries; no direct GI measurement for this specific product has been published. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under HPB Singapore but the record was flagged as ai_generated with grabfood as data_source; nutrient values have been retained as the best available estimate pending laboratory verification. USDA FoodData Central is used as the approved secondary source per Project Veritas v2 source policy. 5. This is a Thai-cuisine dish served in Singapore. Mandatory Thai-language local name is provided per G6 Thai cuisine rule. Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names and aliases are additionally provided to support multilingual retrieval in the Singapore market context, where this product is commercially sold.
